name: Occupant Modern Text
classification_notes: Explicitly positioned within the Didone or modern classification by the foundry, with high contrast strokes and compact spacing adapted from the display-oriented Occupant Modern for text use.
summary: A text-optimised Didone by Cyrus Highsmith, adapted from Occupant Modern with refined spacing and detailing for smaller sizes in print and on screen.
description: Developed by Cyrus Highsmith from his display-oriented Occupant Modern, this text cut adjusts the delicate stroke contrast, proportions and spacing to hold up at running-text sizes across both print and digital media. Marie Otsuka designed the italics and assisted with production, contributing to a five-weight family with matching italics and small caps. Language coverage spans Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek, with a thorough range of figure styles including oldstyle, tabular and superior figures.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Modern Text is part of Cyrus Highsmith's exploration of the Didone or modern classification, adapting the fine details, dimensions and compact spacing of Occupant Modern for text sizes in print and on screen. Marie Otsuka drove the design of the italics and assisted with production.

name: Occupant Modern
classification_notes: Explicitly classified by Occupant as Didone or modern, featuring high contrast strokes derived from a cross-cultural lineage connecting Bodoni to Japanese Mincho design.
summary: A Didone drawn by Cyrus Highsmith and Marie Otsuka, tracing a lineage from Bodoni through Ikko Tanaka's Mincho-style Kocho.
description: The design traces an unusual cross-cultural line: Giambattista Bodoni's 18th century Italian punchcutting, reinterpreted by Japanese graphic designer Ikko Tanaka in his Mincho-style Kocho, and now revisited by Highsmith with italics led by Marie Otsuka. Thirty styles span five weights from Extra Light to Black across standard, Condensed and Compressed widths, each with matching italics. Latin, Cyrillic and Greek script coverage is paired with small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures and localized forms.
foundry_blurb: Occupant describe Occupant Modern as Cyrus Highsmith's exploration of the Didone or modern typeface classification, inspired by Ikko Tanaka's high-contrast Mincho-style typeface Kocho, which was itself drawn from the work of 18th century Italian punchcutter Giambattista Bodoni. Marie Otsuka led the design of the italics and assisted with production. Released in 2025.

name: Carp
classification_notes: Carp is a humanist sans-serif drawing on Excoffon's Antique Olive, characterised by an inverted weight distribution where tops are heavier than bottoms, becoming more pronounced in heavier weights while lighter weights tend toward monolinear.
summary: A humanist sans by Marie Otsuka that takes Excoffon's Antique Olive as its starting point, exaggerating the top-heavy weight distribution across a wide variable family spanning three widths.
description: Carp reworks the inverted stroke contrast of Antique Olive, where letterforms carry more weight at the top than the bottom. In heavier styles this asymmetry is pushed further for expressive display use, while lighter weights settle into a more even monolinear rhythm suited to body text. The family spans 36 static styles across Condensed, Regular and Wide widths with six weights each, all with italics, and is available as a variable font with axes for both weight and width.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Carp as Marie Otsuka's streamlined take on the humanist sans-serif genre, inspired by Excoffon's Antique Olive, where tops are heavier than the bottom parts of letters. The asymmetric weight balance is exaggerated in heavier styles, while lighter styles lean toward a more functional monolinear sans. With variable axes across width and weight, the result is a versatile family for both expressive display and body text settings.

name: Allium Rounded
classification_notes: Derived from Cyrus Highsmith's humanist sans serif Allium, with rounded stroke terminals added by Marie Otsuka; the humanist proportions and structure are preserved throughout.
summary: A rounded variant of Cyrus Highsmith's Allium, adapted by Marie Otsuka with softened stroke endings and recalibrated proportions for headline and display use.
description: Marie Otsuka extended the Allium series by rounding the stroke terminals and adjusting proportions to suit the warmer character of the new design. Five weights from Extra Light to Black, each with italics, cover Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek. The foundry recommends it at headline and desk sizes rather than text settings.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Allium Rounded as grown from Cyrus Highsmith's humanist sans series Allium by Marie Otsuka, who rounded the stroke endings and cultivated the original proportions to suit the new design, with the result working best at headline and desk sizes.

name: Allium
classification_notes: Highsmith describes it explicitly as a humanist sans serif; its broad pan-European character set, calm drawing style and headline orientation confirm a humanist classification rather than geometric or grotesque.
summary: A calm humanist sans by Cyrus Highsmith, drawn for headline and deck sizes with an unusually broad pan-European character set covering Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2017, it runs across six weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, giving twelve styles in total. The drawing is quiet and controlled rather than expressive, suited to editorial headline work where legibility across multiple scripts matters. The family extends into Allium Text and Allium Rounded sub-families, and the glyph set includes old-style figures, tabular figures, ligatures and a full range of stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Allium as a humanist sans serif with a pan-European character set, noting its calm and quiet character and its design intent for headlines and deck sizes. Cyrus Highsmith named the typeface after the genus of flowering plants that includes onions, leeks and garlic.

name: Allium Text
classification_notes: Allium Text is a humanist serif engineered specifically for small text sizes, with accentuated stroke contrast between inside and outside of letterforms to improve legibility at 8 to 24 points.
summary: A text-optimised cut of Cyrus Highsmith's Allium family, tuned for the 8 to 24-point range with accentuated stroke contrast and revised spacing.
description: Distilled from the broader Allium family by Cyrus Highsmith with assistance from June Shin and Cem Eskinazi, Allium Text adjusts proportions and spacing to perform at smaller sizes where the original was not designed to sit. The deliberate increase in contrast between the inside and outside of each letter broadens its legibility range without sacrificing the warmth of the humanist model. Four weights from Light to Black, each with an italic, and thorough coverage across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek make it a capable choice for complex editorial environments.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Allium Text as a version of the Allium family engineered specifically for the 8 to 24-point range. Highsmith adjusted proportions and spacing, and accentuated the contrast between the inside and outside of letters to increase their broadcast range. June Shin and Cem Eskinazi assisted in its development.

name: Antenna Serif
classification_notes: Antenna Serif is explicitly described as the slab counterpart to the sans-serif Antenna, with bracketed serifs applied to a design otherwise sharing Antenna's proportional and structural logic across four widths.
summary: A 56-style slab companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Antenna, spanning seven weights across four widths with matching italics throughout.
description: Developed by Cyrus Highsmith with David Jonathan Ross, Antenna Serif extends the Antenna family into slab territory across Regular, Condensed, Extra Condensed and Compressed widths. Seven weights from Thin to Black run through each width, all with italics, giving designers 56 styles to work across print and digital formats. The consistency of structure across widths makes it a practical choice for publications and systems that need expressive range within a single family.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Antenna Serif as the slab counterpart to Cyrus Highsmith's Antenna, developed with David Jonathan Ross. The family weighs in at 56 styles — seven weights in four widths, each with italics — and is designed to work well in both print and digital formats.

name: Amira 2
classification_notes: Amira occupies the territory between calligraphic tradition and sans-serif structure, drawing comparison to Optima and Lydian. Its angled stress and letterform vitality place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition with a pronounced calligraphic inflection.
summary: A calligraphic sans by Cyrus Highsmith occupying rare ground between Optima's classical structure and Lydian's uninhibited stroke energy, expanded in 2023 with Greek, Cyrillic and small caps by Cem Eskinazi.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2004, Amira 2 sits in the narrow territory between serif-free calligraphy and structured sans, drawing on the lineage of Optima and Lydian while asserting its own angled vitality. Ten styles run from Light to Black with matching italics, supporting small caps and a thorough set of figure styles including oldstyle, tabular and fractions. Cem Eskinazi's 2023 expansion added Greek and Cyrillic scripts alongside small caps, broadening the family for multilingual editorial and text use.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Amira as a rare cross between calligraphy and sans serif, placing it between Zapf's Optima and Warren Chappell's Lydian. Designed by Cyrus Highsmith, it brings angled vitality and fresh rhythmic texture to the page. In 2023, Cem Eskinazi expanded the character set to include small caps plus Greek and Cyrillic coverage.

name: Amira Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface derived from Amira, which sits between calligraphic sans and classical grotesque traditions in the vein of Optima and Lydian; the monospaced constraint imposes fixed-width rhythm on organically calligraphic letterforms.
summary: A monospaced adaptation of Cyrus Highsmith's Amira, drawn by Cem Eskinazi in 2025, imposing fixed-width rhythm on calligraphic letterforms that sit between Optima and Lydian in spirit.
description: Where Amira occupies the unusual territory between calligraphic energy and classical sans structure, Amira Mono takes that organic vitality and subjects it to the mechanical regularity of fixed-width spacing. The result is a typeface with genuine typographic texture: the angled, lively strokes of the original survive the monospaced constraint rather than being flattened by it. Ten styles run from Light to Black with matching italics, and coverage extends across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Amira as claiming adventurous new ground between Hermann Zapf's Optima and Warren Chappell's Lydian, and Amira Mono as Cem Eskinazi's 2025 extension of that territory, imposing a mechanical beat on Amira's organic forms to produce a unique typographic texture.

name: Dispatch Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced derivative of Dispatch, an industrial slab serif; the fixed-width spacing and slab construction place it firmly in the mono category with slab serif character.
summary: A monospaced slab serif by Cyrus Highsmith, spun off from his Dispatch typeface for use on shipping labels and printing PostScript code.
description: Originally made for Highsmith's personal use, Dispatch Mono carries the industrial weight of Dispatch into a fixed-width format suited to technical and utilitarian applications. Four styles cover Regular and Bold with matching italics, and the extended Latin character set handles broad Western European coverage. Its origins in shipping labels and code output give it a purposeful, unfussy quality that suits both screen and print contexts.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Dispatch Mono as a monospaced version of Cyrus Highsmith's industrial strength slab serif, Dispatch, originally created for his personal use on shipping labels and printing PostScript code, now available to all in regular and bold weights with corresponding italics.

name: Antenna
classification_notes: Highsmith describes Antenna as an industrial sans serif, with design emphasis placed on the pattern of white counter shapes rather than on the curves of the letterforms themselves, distinguishing it from humanist or geometric grotesques.
summary: A 56-style industrial sans by Cyrus Highsmith, designed around the negative space formed inside and between letters rather than the conventional focus on stroke curves.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2006, Antenna slows his characteristic fast curves and redirects attention to the rhythm of white counter shapes, producing a typeface that reads as deliberate and composed. Seven weights run across four widths, from regular to compressed, each with matching italics, giving the family broad coverage for editorial, signage and identity work. Stylistic alternates for both upper and lower case characters extend its typographic range further.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Antenna as a versatile industrial sans serif by Cyrus Highsmith that projects confidence and deliberation. Highsmith shifted his focus to the pattern of white shapes formed inside and between characters, slowing his usual fast curves to achieve a considered, structured result. The family offers seven weights across four widths with matching italics, making it suitable for almost any application.

name: Antenna 2
classification_notes: Described explicitly as an industrial sans serif; the design foregrounds the pattern of counter and inter-character white space rather than stroke rhythm, placing it outside humanist or geometric traditions.
summary: An industrial sans serif by Cyrus Highsmith, revisited in 2025 with extended language support, variable fonts and four widths across seven weights.
description: Originally released in 2006, Antenna 2 was drawn with deliberately slowed curves and a focus on the white shapes formed inside and between letters rather than on stroke contrast or optical warmth. The 2025 revision, led by Victoria Rushton, adds Cyrillic, Greek and extended Latin coverage alongside variable font support. Fifty-six static styles spanning Regular, Condensed, Extra Condensed and Compressed widths make it a genuinely broad workhorse for editorial, wayfinding and corporate applications.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Antenna 2 as a versatile industrial sans serif that projects confidence and deliberation. Cyrus Highsmith slowed his usual fast curves and placed emphasis on the pattern of white shapes formed inside and between characters. The 2025 release, led by Victoria Rushton, adds broader language support and variable fonts to the original 2006 design.

name: Gasket Unicase
classification_notes: Gasket Unicase is a display typeface built on a unicase principle, with oversized lowercase forms scaled to match the capitals and no ascenders or descenders, placing it firmly in the display category with a structural quirk that resists standard serif or sans-serif classification.
summary: A unicase display type by Cyrus Highsmith in which oversized lowercase letters are drawn to match capital height, eliminating ascenders and descenders entirely.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2016, Gasket Unicase solves the upper-versus-lower-case dilemma by making it irrelevant: lowercase forms are scaled up to sit alongside capitals without the usual vertical interruptions of ascenders or descenders. Six weights from Thin to Bold each carry an italic, giving reasonable range for a typeface with such a specific structural premise. The result suits display settings where an even, contained texture is more useful than conventional case contrast.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Gasket Unicase as a solution for the typographer who cannot decide which case to use, with Cyrus Highsmith drawing extra-large lowercase forms designed to harmonise with the capitals, the family containing no ascenders or descenders.

name: Dispatch 2
classification_notes: Dispatch 2 is a slab serif drawing from typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels, giving it a utilitarian, industrial character suited to both text and display use.
summary: A wide-ranging industrial slab serif by Cyrus Highsmith, drawn from typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels for heavy-duty text and display use.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and re-released as Dispatch 2 with assistance from Cem Eskinazi, this slab serif takes its cues from the blunt, functional lettering found on engineering drawings, typescript and freight labels. Fifty styles span five widths from Compressed to Wide, each with five weights from Extra Light to Bold and matching italics, covering Latin Extended, Cyrillic and Greek. The result is a workhorse family with enough typographic range to move between text settings and large-scale display work without losing its industrial character.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith created Dispatch in search of an industrial-grade slab serif capable of serving both text and display. It draws on letterforms found in typescript, engineering blueprints and shipping labels. Cem Eskinazi assisted with its re-release as Dispatch 2, intended for heavy commercial use.

name: Gasket Uncial
classification_notes: Gasket Uncial draws directly from the uncial script tradition, a historical calligraphic hand dating to the fourth century and most famously seen in The Book of Kells. Its display-oriented letterforms and historical roots place it firmly in the display category with an uncial subcategory.
summary: Cyrus Highsmith's revival of uncial lettering draws on the calligraphic tradition stretching from the fourth century through to The Book of Kells, rendered across six weights with italics.
description: Rooted in the uncial script tradition and its most celebrated exemplar, The Book of Kells circa 800 CE, Gasket Uncial translates ancient calligraphy into a contemporary type family. Cyrus Highsmith designed six weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, giving the family genuine range for display and editorial use. Extended Latin coverage makes it viable across a broad range of European languages while retaining its distinctly historical character.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Gasket Uncial as Cyrus Highsmith's update of uncial calligraphy, a script dating back to the fourth century and best known through The Book of Kells. Highsmith, drawing on his Irish heritage, designed the family in 2016 as part of the broader Gasket type system alongside Gasket and Gasket Unicase.

name: Heron Serif
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a slab serif counterpart to Heron Sans, with bracketed serifs and sturdy construction suited to both display and text work.
summary: A slab serif by Cyrus Highsmith designed as the companion to Heron Sans, covering five weights with condensed cuts and extended script support across Latin, Cyrillic and Greek.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2010, it pairs directly with Heron Sans as its slab serif counterpart, sharing proportions and rhythm to allow the two families to work together across a publication or identity system. The family runs from Light to Bold with matching italics in both standard and condensed widths, giving twenty styles in total. Cem Eskinazi extended the character set to cover Cyrillic and Greek, making it a strong choice for multilingual editorial and publishing projects.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Heron Serif as the slab serif counterpart to Cyrus Highsmith's Heron Sans, calling it a versatile design capable of performing in both headlines and text. Greek and Cyrillic alphabets were added by Cem Eskinazi.

name: Heron Sans
classification_notes: Mechanically inspired letterforms with added warmth and energy suggest a humanist approach to a utilitarian sans; the mix of headline and text capability points to a well-considered humanist sans rather than a purely geometric or grotesque model.
summary: A mechanically inspired humanist sans by Cyrus Highsmith, balancing utilitarian rigour with enough warmth to work across both text and headline settings.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2008, Heron Sans draws on mechanical letterform principles while retaining the energy needed to hold attention at display sizes and remain readable in running text. The family runs across five weights from Light to Bold, each with an italic, and a full Condensed subfamily adds ten further styles for tighter columns and poster work. Extended Latin coverage and a generous set of OpenType features including stylistic alternates and ligatures make it a capable choice for demanding editorial environments.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Heron Sans as steadfast and no-nonsense, always ready to work. Designer Cyrus Highsmith galvanised its mechanically inspired letters with warmth and energy, resulting in a versatile family capable of performing in both headlines and text settings.

name: Gasket
classification_notes: Gasket displays humanist sans-serif characteristics with a gentle, slightly slanted rhythm and subtly staggered stroke endings that give it an informal, hand-influenced quality distinct from geometric or grotesque models.
summary: A humanist sans by Cyrus Highsmith, originally drawn as a companion to his own children's book illustrations, now expanded into a six-weight family with italics and small caps.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2016, Gasket began as a single font made to sit alongside the designer's own illustrations in a children's book. Its mild slant and subtly staggered stroke endings give the letterforms an easy, animated rhythm without tipping into novelty. The family spans six weights from Thin to Bold, each with a matching italic, and includes small caps alongside Unicase and Uncial variants.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Gasket as having an amiable character, noting that Cyrus Highsmith first drew it as a single font to accompany his illustrations in a children's book. The slight slant and subtly staggered endings give it a gentle hop, and he later added weights, italics and small caps before releasing it more widely.

name: Icebox Magnet
classification_notes: Icebox Magnet is a modular sans serif built from uniform geometric components, inspired by plastic magnetic letters, intended for headlines rather than continuous text.
summary: A modular display sans by Cyrus Highsmith, drawn from the visual language of plastic magnetic refrigerator letters and available in three weights plus a Magnet variant.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2012, the family traces its origins to a box of magnetic plastic letters he bought in 1997. Each character is built from repeating geometric modules that echo the chunky uniformity of those toy letterforms. With three weights and a bonus Magnet style, it suits headlines and short copy where a playful geometric rigour is welcome.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Icebox Magnet as a modular sans serif for headlines and short blurbs of text, available in three weights plus a Magnet style. Its origins lie in a box of magnetic plastic letters Cyrus Highsmith bought at a variety store in 1997, which years later inspired the typeface's geometric, component-based construction. It requires no refrigeration.

name: Ibis Display
classification_notes: Ibis Display draws on Walbaum and Melior as source material, blending classical transitional proportions with Melior's squarish, low-contrast structure; the display version features thinned serifs and tighter fitting suited to larger sizes.
summary: A display serif by Cyrus Highsmith drawing on Walbaum and Melior, offered across three widths and six weights with matching italics.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith in 2009, Ibis Display synthesises Walbaum's classical structure with the squarish geometry of Zapf's Melior, then refines them for display use through thinned serifs and tighter letter-spacing. The family spans Extra Light to Black across upright and standard, Condensed and Compressed widths, totalling 36 styles. A companion text version, Ibis Text, extends its usefulness across continuous reading settings.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith drew inspiration for the Ibis series from an unlikely pairing: Justus Erich Walbaum's 1919 Walbaum and Hermann Zapf's 1952 Melior. For the display version, he thinned the serifs and compacted the fitting, resulting in a wide range of weights and widths, each with corresponding italics.

name: Ibis Text
classification_notes: Ibis Text synthesises the sharp rationalism of Walbaum with the rounded, squared forms of Melior, producing a text serif with both classical structure and mid-century warmth. Its high contrast and bracketed serifs place it firmly in the transitional category.
summary: A text serif drawn by Cyrus Highsmith for his own typography textbook, synthesising Walbaum's rationalism with Melior's rounded geometry across six weights with small caps and italics.
description: Designed originally for Highsmith's book Inside Paragraphs, Ibis Text takes two unlikely reference points, Walbaum's early nineteenth-century rationalist serif and Zapf's 1952 Melior, and fuses them into a highly legible text face. Six weights from Extra Light to Black each carry a corresponding italic and a full small caps set, covering a wide range of editorial and publishing applications. The extended Latin character set and ligature set make it well suited to book typography and long-form reading.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Ibis Text as springing from Cyrus Highsmith's unlikely pairing of Walbaum's 1919 design and Hermann Zapf's 1952 Melior. He drew it for use in his own typography textbook, Inside Paragraphs, and the family comes in a wide range of weights with small caps and corresponding italics throughout.

name: Icebox
classification_notes: Icebox is a modular sans serif derived from the geometric logic of plastic magnetic letters, intended for headlines and short text rather than running copy, placing it firmly in the display category.
summary: A modular display sans by Cyrus Highsmith, drawn from the shapes of plastic magnetic refrigerator letters and available in three weights plus a Magnet variant.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2012, Icebox traces its origins to a box of magnetic letters bought in 1997, whose colourful plastic forms eventually fed into a modular letterform system. Three weights — Light, Regular and Bold — suit headlines and short blurbs, while the bonus Magnet style adds a companion option. Latin Extended character coverage makes it usable across a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Icebox as a modular sans serif for headlines and short text, inspired by the colourful plastic shapes of magnetic letters Cyrus Highsmith picked up from a variety store in 1997. It comes in three weights plus a bonus Magnet style, and requires no refrigeration.

name: Prensa Display
classification_notes: A display-optimised companion to the Prensa text family, with sharper features and tighter fit than its text counterpart, placing it firmly in the display serif category designed for headline and large-scale use.
summary: A sharper, more tightly fitted display companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Prensa text family, built for powerful headline work across three widths.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith as a headline counterpart to his Prensa text face, Prensa Display retains the distinctive internal tension of the original while introducing sharper features and a more compact fit. Thirty-six styles span six weights from Light to Black across standard, Condensed and Compressed widths, each with matching italics. The narrower cuts are built explicitly for dense headline settings where typographic impact matters.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Prensa Display as a companion to Highsmith's popular Prensa text face, sharing its distinctive letterform tension but with sharper features and a more compact fit. Additional narrow widths are included for packing in powerful headlines.

name: Prensa
classification_notes: Prensa draws on the tradition of bookfaces with its deliberate tension between outside and inside curves, a technique rooted in Dwiggins-era transitional serifs such as Electra. The dynamic italics and text-optimised proportions confirm its transitional serif character.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith built around deliberate tension between outer and inner curves, taking cues from Dwiggins's Electra of 1935.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and first released in 1999, Prensa takes its name from the Spanish word for press and is constructed around a technique of wrapping outside curves around the inside, creating visible tension that gives the family its characteristic energy. This tension is most apparent in the italics, which depart from convention to strike a balance between simplicity and vitality. The family runs across six weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, and extends into a Display companion with small caps, oldstyle figures and an extensive Latin character set throughout.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Prensa as a dynamic series for text by Cyrus Highsmith, whose design process of wrapping outside curves around the inside creates deliberate tension between the two — a technique they trace to Electra, the 1935 bookface by W.A. Dwiggins. That tension, they note, is especially visible in the italics, which move away from traditional forms to balance simplicity with vitality.

name: Quiosco
classification_notes: Quiosco draws on the tradition of W.A. Dwiggins, exploring contrast between the inside and outside of letterforms. Its lively texture and narrow-column suitability place it firmly in the humanist serif category.
summary: A text serif by Cyrus Highsmith rooted in W.A. Dwiggins's exploration of letterform contrast, optimised for narrow columns and compact wordspacing.
description: Designed in 2007, Quiosco takes its name from the Spanish word for kiosk and forms part of Highsmith's sustained study of W.A. Dwiggins and the tension between a letter's interior and exterior shapes. Three weights from Regular to Bold, each with an italic, give the family range for editorial hierarchies. Its lively texture holds readability in narrow measures where many text faces falter.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Quiosco as a text family designed by Cyrus Highsmith, part of his exploration of the work of W.A. Dwiggins and the contrast between the inside and outside of letterforms. The family brings a lively texture to narrow columns of text and permits compact wordspaces without sacrificing readability.

name: Salvo Serif
classification_notes: Salvo Serif is explicitly described as a slab design, with heavy serifs that form unified word shapes. The pronounced serifs and sturdy construction place it squarely in the slab serif category.
summary: A slab serif by Cyrus Highsmith designed to unify three different magazines under a single typographic system, offered in five weights and three widths with italics throughout.
description: Conceived as a system-level solution for editorial publishing, Salvo Serif spans five weights from Light to Black across regular, condensed and extra condensed widths, each with corresponding italics — 30 styles in total. The heavy serifs are deliberately assertive, building cohesive word shapes that pull the reader's eye. Designed in 2011 alongside the companion Salvo Sans, the family was built to hold together across varied editorial contexts without losing its distinctive character.
foundry_blurb: Cyrus Highsmith created the extensive Salvo series to unify three different magazines under one typographic umbrella. Salvo Serif is an outspoken slab design in five weights and three widths with corresponding italics. The heavy serifs help to form unified words that catch the reader's eye and draw them into the text.

name: Quiosco Display
classification_notes: Quiosco Display is a high-contrast serif adapted for headline use, with sharpened, stone-cut forms that emphasise contrast over the softer clay-like qualities of the text original, placing it firmly in the transitional to high-contrast serif tradition.
summary: A display-optimised redrawing of Cyrus Highsmith's Quiosco, reinterpreted by Cem Eskinazi with sharpened, stone-cut contrast for headline use.
description: Where Quiosco was drawn with a softness suited to text sizes, Eskinazi's Display adaptation introduces crisper, more chiselled forms that perform at large scale. The weight range is extended beyond the text version, running from Regular to Black with matching italics throughout, and a dedicated set of figures accompanies the small caps. Latin Extended coverage and a full suite of ligatures, oldstyle figures and stylistic alternates make it a complete tool for editorial and typographic display work.
foundry_blurb: Occupant describe Quiosco Display as Cem Eskinazi sharpening Highsmith's textface for headlines, imagining the transformation from something made of clay into something chiselled in stone, with an extended weight range and a special set of figures drawn to accompany the small caps.

name: Salvo Sans
classification_notes: Described as energetic and optimistic with a broad width range and italics, characteristics typical of humanist sans-serif design intended for editorial and text use across varied sizes.
summary: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith for editorial use, this five-weight, three-width sans was built to unify multiple magazines under a single typographic system.
description: Commissioned to bring typographic coherence to three separate magazines, it spans five weights from Light to Black across standard, Condensed and Extra Condensed widths, each with matching italics. The thirty-style family is positioned for headlines, pull quotes and short text settings, with a broad Latin Extended character set supporting a wide range of Western and Central European languages. The design carries an upbeat energy suited to fast-paced editorial environments without sacrificing legibility at smaller sizes.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Salvo Sans as an energetic, optimistic design created by Cyrus Highsmith as part of the broader Salvo series, originally developed to unify three magazines under one typographic system. It offers five weights and three widths with corresponding italics, working across headlines, pull quotes and short text settings.

name: Scout
classification_notes: Scout is a grotesque sans with industrial DIN-adjacent proportions, tempered by references to Venus and Cairoli, placing it firmly in the European grotesque tradition rather than geometric or humanist camps.
summary: A DIN-influenced grotesque by Cyrus Highsmith that tempers industrial rigour with references to Venus and Cairoli across six weights, two widths and full italics.
description: Drawn by Cyrus Highsmith in 2018, Scout answers the perennial demand for a DIN-like sans without the coldness of the original, folding in traits from Venus and Cairoli alongside Highsmith's own graphic instincts. The family runs from Thin to Black with italic companions throughout, and a Condensed subfamily doubles the range for headlines, captions and sidebars. Greek and Cyrillic alphabets, contributed by June Shin, extend its reach across multilingual publishing.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Scout as Cyrus Highsmith's response to the recurring request for an industrial sans that resembles DIN 1451 without being too close to it, blending influences from Venus and Cairoli with his own sensibilities. The family covers a wide range of weights and two widths plus italics, suited to headlines, captions and sidebars, with Greek and Cyrillic added by June Shin.

name: Scout Text
classification_notes: Scout Text is a humanist sans optimised for text setting, companion to the display-oriented Scout, with legibility-focused adjustments including tails on 'l' and 'a' that bring it closer to humanist conventions.
summary: A text-optimised companion to Cyrus Highsmith's Scout, tuned for small sizes through increased x-height, open spacing and disambiguating tails on the 'l' and 'a'.
description: Drawn by Cyrus Highsmith in 2016 as a counterpart to his display-oriented Scout sans, Scout Text makes targeted modifications for body text use. The x-height is raised, spacing is opened out, and tails are added to the lowercase 'l' and 'a' to improve legibility at small sizes. Four weights from Light to Black are offered, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Scout Text as drawn to accompany the popular utilitarian sans Scout, optimised for small sizes by increasing the x-height, opening the spacing, and adding tails to the lowercase 'l' and 'a'. It comes in four weights plus italics.

name: Zócalo Display
classification_notes: Zócalo Display is an oldstyle serif inspired by Nicholas Kis's historical models, adapted for headline use with compact proportions and high contrast suited to display sizes.
summary: A compact oldstyle display serif by Cyrus Highsmith, originally drawn for the 2002 redesign of Mexico City newspaper El Universal.
description: Designed by Cyrus Highsmith and released in 2005, Zócalo Display draws on Nicholas Kis's oldstyle models filtered through Highsmith's impressions of México. It was created as part of Eduardo Danilo's editorial redesign of El Universal, one of Mexico City's leading newspapers, and is tuned for headlines and decks with a sparkling, tight character. The family spans four weights from Regular to Black, each with an italic, and sits alongside optical-size companions Zócalo Text and Zócalo Banner.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Display as part of Cyrus Highsmith's Zócalo series, developed for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal. The design takes inspiration from Nicholas Kis's oldstyle as well as Highsmith's impressions of México itself, resulting in a sparkling, compact face built for headlines and decks.

name: Zócalo Banner
classification_notes: Rooted in Nicholas Kis's oldstyle tradition, Zócalo Banner is a display-oriented serif with simplified serifs designed for headlines and decks, placing it firmly in the oldstyle serif subcategory at larger sizes.
summary: A headline variant of the Zócalo series, designed by Cyrus Highsmith for the 2002 redesign of Mexico City newspaper El Universal, with simplified serifs suited to large-size setting.
description: Zócalo Banner is one of three optical-size members of Highsmith's Zócalo series, originally commissioned for Eduardo Danilo's redesign of El Universal. Drawing on Nicholas Kis's oldstyle as a historical anchor, it adapts those forms through Highsmith's impressions of México itself. Simplified serifs distinguish it from Zócalo Display, making it the workhorse for headlines and decks where optical refinement matters.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Banner as a stylistic variation of Zócalo Display, designed by Cyrus Highsmith as part of his Zócalo series created for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal, the leading Mexico City newspaper. The design was inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and by Highsmith's own impressions of México, with Banner's simplified serifs tailored specifically for headlines and decks.

name: Zócalo Text
classification_notes: Zócalo Text draws explicitly from Chauncey Griffith's Ionic No.5, a classic newspaper-optimised serif, giving it the sturdy low-contrast proportions characteristic of ionic newsfaces. The oldstyle influences of Nicholas Kis also inform its character.
summary: A sturdy text serif by Cyrus Highsmith, rooted in Ionic newsface tradition and originally commissioned for the 2002 redesign of Mexico City's El Universal.
description: Designed in 2006 as the text-setting member of the Zócalo series, this face derives its compact, high-legibility proportions from Chauncey Griffith's Ionic No.5 while drawing character from Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and Highsmith's own impressions of México. The family runs to four styles — Regular, Italic, Bold and Bold Italic — with small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures and standard ligatures included. Part of a series that also extends to display and banner optical sizes.
foundry_blurb: Occupant Fonts describe Zócalo Text as part of Cyrus Highsmith's Zócalo series, created for Eduardo Danilo's 2002 redesign of El Universal, a leading Mexico City newspaper. The text cut takes its sturdy proportions from Chauncey Griffith's classic newsface Ionic No.5, while the broader series was inspired by Nicholas Kis's oldstyle and Highsmith's impressions of México itself.
